title: Observe early vs late binding
prompt_md:
## Goal

A non-virtual call is resolved at compile time from the *static* type (early binding); a virtual call is resolved at runtime from the *actual* object type (late binding). Build a hierarchy where one function of each kind is observed through a `const Base&`, and watch them disagree.

## Classes

- `Account` is the base. `kind()` is a non-virtual function returning `account`. `rate()` is a virtual function returning `1`.
- `Savings` derives from `Account`: it has its own `kind()` returning `savings`, and it overrides `rate()` to return `5`.
- `Checking` derives from `Account`: it has its own `kind()` returning `checking`, and it overrides `rate()` to return `2`.

## Input

- Line 1: integer `N` (1 <= N <= 6).
- Next `N` lines: either `savings` or `checking`.

## Output

For each line, create the matching derived object and bind a `const Account&` to it. Through that reference print:
```
Kind: <kind()>
Rate: <rate()>
```
Because `kind()` is non-virtual it always resolves to `Account::kind` (printing `account`); because `rate()` is virtual it resolves to the derived override.

For input `2` / `savings` / `checking`:
```
Kind: account
Rate: 5
Kind: account
Rate: 2
```

test_cases.label: Savings then checking
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Kind: account
Rate: 5
Kind: account
Rate: 2

test_cases.label: Two savings then checking
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Kind: account
Rate: 5
Kind: account
Rate: 5
Kind: account
Rate: 2

test_cases.label: Mixed four
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Kind: account
Rate: 2
Kind: account
Rate: 2
Kind: account
Rate: 5
Kind: account
Rate: 2

title: Polymorphic dispatch with mixed types
prompt_md:
## Goal

An early-bound (non-virtual) function can itself call a late-bound (virtual) one. When you invoke the non-virtual function through a base pointer, the base version of it runs (early binding), but the virtual call inside it still dispatches to the derived override (late binding). Build a logging hierarchy that shows both effects on a single line.

## Classes

- `LogEntry` is the base:
  - `severity()` is non-virtual and returns `INFO`.
  - `text()` is virtual and returns `generic`.
  - `render()` is non-virtual and prints `<severity()>: <text()>` followed by a newline.
- `Warning` derives from `LogEntry`: it has its own `severity()` returning `WARN` and overrides `text()` to return `warning`.
- `Error` derives from `LogEntry`: it has its own `severity()` returning `ERR` and overrides `text()` to return `error`.

## Input

- Line 1: integer `N` (1 <= N <= 8).
- Next `N` lines: either `warning` or `error`.

## Output

Store the entries through `LogEntry*` and call `render()` on each through that base pointer. Because `render()` and `severity()` are non-virtual the base versions run (so severity is always `INFO`); because `text()` is virtual the derived text is used.

For input `2` / `warning` / `error`:
```
INFO: warning
INFO: error
```

test_cases.label: Two warnings then error
test_cases.expected_stdout:
INFO: warning
INFO: warning
INFO: error

test_cases.label: Mixed four
test_cases.expected_stdout:
INFO: error
INFO: warning
INFO: error
INFO: warning
